card.intuition: A browser security mechanism that allows or blocks a frontend app on one domain from accessing resources on a different domain.
card.analogy: A school dance where only students from that school are allowed in, unless the principal (the server) explicitly adds an guest list (CORS headers) allowing students from the rival school.
when_to_use: When your frontend (e.g., app.com) needs to fetch data from your API (e.g., api.app.com)
Allowing third-party widgets or scripts to access your public data
Preventing malicious sites from making unauthorized requests on behalf of your users
gotchas: CORS is enforced by the BROWSER, not the server — a server might successfully process a request but the browser hides the result
Simple requests (GET/POST) behave differently than requests with custom headers, which require an 'OPTIONS' preflight check

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
Browser detects cross-origin request and sends OPTIONS preflight.
Preflight asks: can Origin A use method X with headers Y?
Server responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin if origin permitted.
Browser sends actual request only if preflight was approved.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the required server-side CORS headers to allow requests from https://myapp.com.
hints: The 'Origin' header specifies which domain is allowed
The 'Headers' header specifies which custom client headers are allowed
exercise.test_code:
assert response_headers["Access-Control-Allow-Origin"] == "https://myapp.com"
assert "Headers" in [k.split("-")[-1] for k in response_headers.keys()]
print("PASS")
